step1Contents.navDescription: Two regulatory questions for the thyroid case study. Choose one to explore or scroll for an overview.

questions.label: Question 1: Compound Information
questions.description: What information about silychristin do we need to give as an advice to women in their early pregnancy to decide whether the substance can be used?

questions.label: Question 2
questions.description: Does silychristin influence the thyroid hormone-mediated brain development in the fetus resulting in cognitive impairment in children?

content.description: Thyroid hormones (THs), mainly thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3), are essential regulators of neurological processes and play a crucial role in fetal brain development. Deficiencies in TH levels during critical periods of neurodevelopment have been associated with severe and irreversible neurodevelopmental problems later in life. TH concentrations in the brain are therefore carefully maintained through negative feedback loops and local control mechanisms, including thyroid hormone transmembrane transporters, which regulate cellular influx and efflux; deiodinases, which regulate the activation and inactivation of THs; and thyroid hormone receptors, which bind to TH-responsive elements, thereby regulating the transcription of TH-responsive genes. This complex regulatory system can be disrupted by chemicals that interfere with TH signaling, potentially resulting in severe neurodevelopmental problems.

content.section: Safety Assessment of Thyroid Hormone System Disrupting Chemicals
content.description: Internationally validated and accepted OECD test methods for TH system disrupting chemicals mainly consist of animal studies, which often only assess TH system disrupting effects through histopathological changes in the thyroid gland or changes in serum TH levels. However, TH system disrupting effects are not always reflected in these endpoints and these regulatory test methods currently lack the ability to test for specific molecular mechanisms of TH system disrupting effects. Moreover, species differences between rodents and humans hamper the evaluation of chemical safety for humans through the use of animal studies.

This case study focuses on assessing the neurodevelopmental effects of TH system disrupting chemicals. The aim of the case study was to develop a human based safety assessment for potential TH system disrupting chemicals that are difficult to assess using animal models. Two regulatory questions were formulated to demonstrate how the VHP can be used. In regulatory question 1 the vital information for regulatory bodies to advice women in their early pregnancy on the use of silychristin is gathered. Regulatory question 2 aims to develop a safety assessment for silychristin by integrating in silico and in vitro methods developed in the VHP.

Q2.navDescription: Follow these steps for the regulatory question: Does silychristin influence the thyroid hormone-mediated brain development in the fetus resulting in cognitive impairment in children? Click a button or scroll for more info.

content.section: Silychristin and the thyroid hormone system
content.description: Silychristin is one of the main constituents of the mixture silymarin, a crude extract obtained from the seeds of Milk Thistle (Silybum Marianum). Silymarin is one of the most widely used dietary herbal supplements in the world and is often sold as a hepatoprotective agent or breast-feeding supplement. Various studies have shown that silychristin is a potent and highly selective inhibitor of MCT8, a crucial thyroid hormone transmembrane transporter regulating TH transport into the brain. In humans, MCT8-deficiency has been associated with a severe neurodevelopment disorder known as Allan-Herndon Dudley syndrome. Silychristin is often used a model compound in scientific research to study the effects of MCT8 inhibition, due to the potency and selectivity towards MCT8. However, despite the widespread use as a supplement no safety evaluations for human exposure have been conducted for silychristin.

It is known that rodents are less sensitive to the neurodevelopmental effects caused by MCT8-deficiency compared to human patients. The current regulatory test methods for TH system disrupting chemicals therefore cannot properly assess the neurodevelopmental effects of specific MCT8-inhibiting chemicals, such as silychristin. Additionally, the lack of brain-specific endpoints in current regulatory test methods further hamper the evaluation of MCT8-inhibition of TH-mediated neurodevelopment. The use of the VHP4Safety platform might circumvent these issues by using a human based assessment, through the integration of in silico and in vitro models, to assess the effects of silychristin exposure on TH-mediated brain development in the fetus.

The scope of this case study was mainly on the direct acute effects of silychristin on neurodevelopment in the fetus. Therefore, the influence of maternally derived TH perturbations are not yet taken into account for the risk assessment. We first derived a realistic exposure scenario for silychristin and subsequently used modelling approaches to determine how much silychristin reaches the fetal brain. Then a combination of in silico and in vitro models were employed to assess the potential hazard of silychristin. A bioactivity-exposure response (BER) was calculated to determine if silychristin concentrations in the fetal brain can interfere with MCT8-mediated TH transport. The predicted fetal brain silyhchristin concentrations were then used as input in a quantitative AOP (qAOP) to assess whether a realistic exposure to silychristin could trigger key events that might lead neurodevelopmental adverse effects. Finally, we compare the results from the qAOP to whole-organism adverse effects derived from in vivo data. An overview of the safety assessment workflow can be found in figure 1. Figure 1. An overview of the process flow of the thyroid hormone mediated developmental neurotoxicity safety assessment workflow for silychristin exposure through dietary herbal supplements. The safety assessment workflow steps are shown in purple with the case study specific (sub)steps shown in dashed green boxes. The blue boxes indicate the different methods used for the case study steps are shown in blue.

content.section: Establishing a realistic exposure scenario for Silychristin
content.description: Silychristin is most commonly sold as part of the mixture silymarin present in Milk thistle Extracts, that are sold as dietary herbal supplements. The mayor route of silychristin exposure in humans will be therefore through oral ingestion of herbal supplements. To determine a realistic exposure scenario for silychristin, we therefore looked for various information sources that report on the intake of these herbal supplements, such as existing regulatory information and prescribed dosing recommendations from product leaflets. Subsequently, we performed a mixture analysis of silymarin mixtures to derive the associated silychristin content. This mixture analysis was based on literature data and experimental analysis of silymarin products. We then used this information to calculate a realistic exposure scenario of silychristin for women in their early pregnancies.

• Input:Estimated daily intake of Silychristin
  • Output:Silychristin exposure calculation

steps.label: Prediction of maternal systemic and fetail brain concentration
steps.value: Prediction of maternal systemic and fetail brain concentration

content.section: Prediction of maternal systemic concentration and concentration at MIE
content.description: From an oral exposure, silychristin has to travel through the gut into the systemic circulation and towards the brain to exert a neurological effect. The local concentration of silychristin in the brain will eventually determine the toxic effect of silychristin. It is therefore imperative to predict how much silychristin will reach the brain. We employed physiological based kinetic (PBK) modelling to derive fetal brain concentration of silychristin using the estimated RDI’s from the exposure scenario. Literature data and in vitro data was used to parameterize the model.

• Input:Silychristin physicochemical and kinetic properties
  • Output:Predicted maternal systemic concentration of Silychristin

content.section: How can chemical exposure cause thyroid hormone disruption mediated developmental neurotoxicity?
content.description: Adverse outcome pathways (AOPs) are biological pathways describing how effects on molecular targets can lead to adverse outcomes at a biological level of organization relevant to risk assessment. For this case study, a brain specific AOP was developed to describe the modulation of fetal TH homeostasis and how this interacts with biological targets leading to an adverse outcome. Using this AOP, we identified molecular initiation events (MIEs) and further downstream key events (KEs) relevant for thyroid hormone disruption mediated developmental neurotoxicity.

content.section: The thyroid hormone mediated developmental neurotoxicity AOP
content.description:
Based on literature data and molecular AOP mapping, a thyroid hormone mediated developmental neurotoxicity AOP was developed as depicted in figure 1. The MIEs underlying thyroid hormone disruption mediated developmental neurotoxicity can be grouped in three categories describing the effects of chemicals on TH transport (MCT8, OATP1C1 and other transporters), deiodinase (D2&3) activity and TH receptor (TRα1) activation. TRα1 is both a MIE as a further downstream key event in neuronal and oligodendrocyte cells. From reduced TRα1 activation, following key events include a decrease in BDNF expression, reduced synaptogenesis and reduced neuronal network formation ultimately leading to decreased cognitive function.

Figure 1. A schematic overview of the adverse outcome pathways for thyroid hormone mediated developmental neurotoxicity during pregnancy.

• Input:AOP data
  • Output:placeholder

steps.label: Silychristin in vivo data
steps.description: Relating silychristin effects to whole organism data

content.section: How do the effects seen from in vitro and in silico compare to adverse outcomes in the organism?
content.description: Understanding the biological effects of compounds like silychristin requires bridging the gap between in vitro findings and real-world organismal responses. Zebrafish, rodent and human clinical data are used to relate the effects of silychristin in the safety assessment workflow to observable adverse outcomes at the organism level. This comparison serves to validate the NAM-derived predictions, identifying discrepancies, and ensures our safety assessment workflow aligns with real-world biological responses.

• Input: Case study predictions from exposure, kinetics and AOP blocks
  • Output: silychristin effects on organism level data (zebrafish, clinical data) to contextualize NAM prediction to in vivo effects

steps.label: Silychristin daily intake from measured products
steps.description: Derived from analytically measured silymarin products

content.section: Existing information from European regulatory bodies
content.description: Currently, no assessment on the safety of Silybum marianum has been prepared by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA). There is an herbal monograph on Silybum marianum (L.) Gaertn., fructus for traditional use as a herbal medicine by European Medicines Agency (EMA). There are different types of extracts registered as herbal medicine for traditional use and the posology of these herbal medicines is dependent on the type of extract. This only refers to traditional herbal medicines and not to herbal preparations (i.e. food supplements and herbal tea). Silybum marianum is available on the Dutch market as both, including powder for tea.

content.section: Information from product leaflets of herbal supplements.
content.description: Recommended use and dose information for food supplements and herbal tea can be found on leaflets and additional information as presented in web shops. Therefore, an online Google search was conducted on the words ‘mariadistel’ and ‘silybum marianum’ to identify web shops that sell food supplements containing Silybum marianum or powder to make herbal tea on the Dutch market. These web shops were visited to find use and dosing information on these food supplements. Additionally, leaflets are published and gathered in external databases, such as the NIH Dietary Supplement label database. These food supplements are intended for US markets, but many manufacturers also produce supplements for European markets. Information from over 100 leaflets from this database have been gathered to extract dosing information of silymarin supplements. This information can be found in the dose recommendations from product leaflets dataset. The recommended daily dose for silymarin derived from product leaflets on the Dutch Market varies from 15 to 400 mg. Also the recommended dose for silymarin per serving varies from 15 to 400 mg (due to differences between food supplements). The recommended daily dose for silymarin derived from product leaflets found in the DSLD varies from 8 to 840 mg. The recommended dose for silymarin per serving varies from 4 to 480 mg.

Flavonolignan contents can vary considerably between silymarin mixtures. Most product information of gathered silymarin supplements only report on the amount of silymarin itself and not on silychristin. In order to derive estimated intake of silycrhistin it was therefore necessary to estimate the amount of silychristin in silymarin mixtures. Therefore, a literature search was conducted using Sysrev to gather publications that analytically measured contents of commercially available silymarin capsules. The literature search included various combinations of the following search terms: “Silymarin”, “capsules”, “contents”, “constituents”, “active components”, “flavonolignan”, “mixture analysis”. The selection criteria of literature were based on relevance to the scientific questions and quality selection was done based on expert judgement. Reported silychristin contents in silymarin mixtures varied between literature sources, but could be categorized in three orders: low silychristin contents (~6% of total silymarin), average silychristin contents (~17% of total silymarin) and high silychristin contents (~31% of total silymarin). An overview of the gathered data from literature can be found in mixture analysis of silymarin products. Additionally, a selection of silymarin products were purchased from the Dutch Market and analytically measured using liquid chromotography-tandem mass spectrometry to compare the composition of silymarin mixtures derived from literature to commercially available silymarin products. This data can be found in the file Silychristin daily intake from measured products

content.section: Calculating a realistic exposure scenario for silychristin intake
content.description:
The estimated daily oral intake of silychristin was calculated by combining the mixture analysis of silymarin mixtures with the calculated recommended daily dose from product leaflets. The oral intake of silychristin was calculated in mg/kg/bw per day, assuming a pregnant woman at early pregnancy has a body weight close to the average women (64.1 kg, te Biesebeek et al., 2014). An overview of the estimated daily oral intake for silychristin products from the DSLD and from the Dutch Market can be found in figure 1.

Figure 1. The estimated RDI’s from silychristin-containing supplements derived from product leaflets in the DSLD and the Dutch Market. Each dot represents a single product for which the daily intake of silychristin was estimated for a low (red), moderate (green) and high (blue) silychristin product content. Summary statistics describe the mean, lowest (min) and highest (max) daily intake from the respective datasets. N = total amount products in each dataset.

In addition to the estimated oral daily intake, we also experimentally measured the silychristin contents of three commercially available silymarin products. Using the experimentally measured silychristin contents, we then calculated RDI’s for these product using the same method as for the estimated oral daily intake from the DSLD and Dutch Market dataset. The recommended dose, product weight, silychristin content and daily intake for the experimentally measured products can be found in table 1. Overall, the daily intake calculated from the experimentally measured products fell in the range of the moderate estimated daily oral intake range from the DSLD dataset and in the high estimated daily oral intake range from the Dutch Market dataset. Although, both the mean and max estimated daily oral take from both datasets were higher than the daily intake of the experimentally measured products, suggesting that we are slightly over-estimating the oral intake of silychristin.

Table 1. Calculated daily intake of silychristin in mg/kg/bw from purchased silymarin dietary supplements.

productdosetotal weightsilychristin (in mg/g product)daily dose silychristin (mg)RDI (mg/kg/bw)

• Input:Literature-based exposure information
  • Output:Realistic Silychristin exposure calculation

Prediction of maternal systemic and feta.navTitle: Prediction of maternal systemic and fetail brain concentration - Tools and Workflow (Q2)

steps.label: Human pharmacokinetic data
steps.description: Overview of human pharmacokinetic data of silychristin

content.description: A silychristin specific PBK model was constructed from a publicly available silybin based PBK model under the assumption that silychristin behaves similarly to silybin. The PBK model was built in R* and uses the rxode2 package to solve differential equations in the model. The PBK model was completely parameterized using in silico prediction tools and literature data, except for the gut absorption which was measured experimentally. The PBK model was validated used human pharmacokinetic data derived from literature.

The permeability of silychristin and silymarin across the human gut was experimentally measured using the inTESTine™ system, a two-compartment barrier model using ex vivo human jejunum tissue. Transport of pure silychristin compound and silychirstin when present in silymarin mixture over the human jejunum was assessed using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrophotometry and expressed as an apparent permeability (Papp) value. The Papp values from the pure silychristin compound were used to calculate a gut absorption factor using a well-stirred absorption-disposition method (Usanky et al. 2005). The PBK model was then reparametrized using this gut absorption factor.

content.section: Validation of the silychristin PBK model
content.description: Human pharmacokinetic data was obtained from three clinical studies in which blood plasma concentrations of silychristin were measured after 24 hours after oral administration of silymarin capsules. The PBK model was ran on a similar silychristin oral intake and exposure window as described in these clinical studies. The reported maximal blood concentration (Cmax) was compared to the predicted PBK Cmax from our model before and after reparameterization of the gut absorption. An overview of the reported and predicted Cmax values can be found in table 1. The predicted Cmax after adjusting the PBK model with the experimentally derived gut absorption factor closely resembled the reported Cmax, within a 5-fold difference.

content.section: Modelling exposure scenarios with the PBK model
content.description:
We modelled the maximal fetal brain concentration (CfMax) with the adjusted siychristin PBK model using the previously estimated RDI’s of both the DSLD and the Dutch Market dataset as input data. We used an exposure window of two weeks with dose every 24 hours, which is the maximal recommended dosing regime provided on product leaflets. The resulting distributions, mean, 5th and 95th percentiles of the predicted CfMax in each exposure scenario (low, mid, high) for both the DSLD and Dutch Market dataset can be found in figure 1.

Figure 1. Distribution of the maximal predicted fetal brain concentrations (CfMax) of the DSLD and Dutch Market datasets. Distributions are shown for each exposure scenario indicating low (red), moderate (green) and high (blue) silychristin content of the supplements. Added tables contain the summary statistics describing the mean, 5th and 95th percentiles for the distributions. Each exposure scenario contained 114 datapoints for the DSLD and 8 data points for the Dutch Market distribution.

• Input: Computational models
  • Output:Prediction data

steps.label: hNPT transcriptomic data
steps.description: Effects of altered TH levels on hNPT

content.section: Development on AOP based on literature
content.description: An AOP for thyroid hormone disruption mediated developmental neurotoxicity was developed from published literature collected from Pubmed and Scopus databases. The literature search was conducted based various combinations of thyroid hormone related search terms as described in Dierichs et al. 2025.

A molecular AOP was constructed by mapping the thyroid hormone disruption mediated developmental neurotoxicity AOP framework to molecular pathways using WikiPathways. Transcriptomic data from the human neural progenitor test (hNPT), where human neural progenitor cells are exposed to low and high T3 concentrations, was then integrated with this molecular AOP. Using BridgeDB, differentially expressed genes were mapped to biological process representing key events in the thyroid hormone disruption mediated developmental neurotoxicity AOP. This integration of transcriptomic data with molecular AOP allows for the identification of genes associated with key events within the AOP that are significantly perturbed by disrupting thyroid hormone homeostasis.

• Input:AOP data
  • Output:Evidence-based refined AOP

steps.value: Establishing in vitro point of departure

content.section: Characterization of silychristin exposure to MIE activation
content.description: Molecular initiation events (MIEs) describe the interaction between chemicals and biological targets at the molecular level. Activation of these MIEs by a chemical can start a cascade of key events that ultimately lead to adverse effect. The thyroid hormone disruption mediated developmental neurotoxicity AOP described in the previous step has identified several MIEs that could trigger the AOP. A combination of computational prediction tools and in vitro assays were used to assess which MIEs are triggered upon exposure to silychristin. Concentration-dependent effects of silychristin were established for these MIEs to derive in vitro PoD for relevant MIEs.

• Input:Silychristin exposure
  • Output:Experimental and computational evidence of silychristin-mediated MIE perturbation

steps.label: Effect on KLF9 and BDNF gene expression

content.section: Characterization of downstream key event effects of silychristin exposure
content.description: A neuronal stem cell model, called the human neural progenitor test (hNPT), was used to further investigate the effect of decreasing TH concentrations on downstream key events in the neurodevelopmental AOP. Human embryonic stem cells were differentiated into a neuron-astrocyte coculture. Gene expression analysis using qPCR was performed on the hNPT after exposure to different concentrations of T3 to measure the expression of KLF9 and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) expression. BDNF is a neurotropin that is essential for neuronal differentiation and plays a key role in synaptogenesis of neurons. Additionally, neuronal network activity was assessed in neuron-astrocyte coculture models using a multi-electrode array. The data generated from these in vitro assays served as the foundation to build a quantitative adverse ourcome pathway (qAOP) model.

• Input:Neuron-astrocyte model measurements
  • Output:Mechanistic evidence linking silychristin to neurodevelopmental outcomes

steps.label: Bioactivity-exposure ratio
steps.description: Comparison between in vitro PoDs and fetal brain concentrations

content.description:
Bioactivity-Exposure Ratios (BERs) compare an in vitro bioactivity to a realistic exposure concentration to assess whether exposure to a compound can trigger MIEs. For silychristin, we calculated BERs by dividing MCT8/OATP1C1 PoDs from in vitro TH transport inhibition assays by fetal brain concentrations derived from the PBK modelling. A BER < 1 would imply that silychristin concentrations in the fetal brain could inhibit TH transport, potentially leading to adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes. Conversely, a BER > 1 suggests exposure is below the bioactive threshold and that silychristin most likely does not inhibit TH transport at realistic exposures. We randomly sampled from the in vitro PoD distributions of the MCT8 and OATP1C1 datasets and compared this to randomly sampled fetal brain concentrations in each exposure scenario. This resulted in a distribution of possible BERs from 10.000 combinations of randomly sampled in vitro PoD and fetal brain concentration. These BER distributions for both the DSLD and Dutch Market product data set are shown in figure 1. Overall, the results indicate that MCT8-mediated TH transport is predicted to be inhibited at realistic exposures of silychristin from both DSLD and Dutch Market products. OATP1C1-mediated TH transport is not predicted to be affected at realistic exposure scenarios of silychristin. As realistic exposures of silychristin can inhibit TH transport is it necessary to study how silychristin exposure effects further downstream key events.

Figure 1. Distribution of the BER for MCT8 (left) and OATP1C1 (right) activation from realistic fetal brain concentrations of silychristin. Data shown the distribution for each exposure scenario of low, moderate and high amount of silychristin percentages per product. The black dashed line indicates a BER of 1.

content.section: Modelling silychristin effect on downstream key events using quantitative AOP
content.description: A quantitative adverse outcome pathways (qAOP) model was used to predict whether the derived silychristin concentrations in the fetal brain after maternal exposure can form a potential risk to develop neurodevelopmental adverse effects. A qAOP provides a mathematical description of the MIE and downstream key events, which can be used to predict the possibility of an adverse outcome after chemical exposure. An ordinary differential equations (ODE) -based qAOP model was used to predict the time dependent effect of silychristin exposure to inhibition of TH uptake and the possibility to trigger downstream key events of KLF9 expression and synaptogenesis. The qAOP model demonstrated that silychristin initially inhibits the T3 accumulation in neuronal cells an causes a decrease in the expression of KLF9, however this effect quickly wanes and KLF9 expression was restored to control levels after 24 hours (Figure 2.). Silychristin was also predicted to have a marginal effect on synaptogenesis, only slightly decreasing the number of electrode bursts. Overall, these data indicate that while silychristin may inhibit the TH transport in neuronal cells, the effect on downstream KE is less severe and might not be significantly altered.

• Input:qAOP
  • Output:Predicted inhibition of TH uptake by Silychristin

steps.label: QSPRpred
steps.description: Chemical activity prediction from QSAR models

content.description: Multiple in silico tools were run to predict the interaction between silychristin and MIEs. The models were selected based on their relevance to the TH-specific MIEs and on their free public availability. All in silico tools were ran with the SMILES “COC1=C(C=CC(=C1)[C@H]2[C@@H](C3=C(O2)C(=CC(=C3)[C@@H]4[C@H](C(=O)C5=C(C=C(C=C5O4)O)O)O)O)CO)O”. The following in silico tools were selected for this case study.

The QSPRpred app is designed to predict the activity of chemicals of interest to various biological targets. This prediction is done by target-specific, pretrained quantitative structure activity (QSAR) models. The QSPRpred app contains two models that have been used to predict the effect of silychristin on TRα1 and TRβ1.

The OECD QSAR toolbox AI assistant (O-QT) is a multi-agent AI system that automates the process of chemical analysis, hazard assessment, and read-across strategy development utilizing the OECD QSAR toolbox. It can generate concise report from running multiple prediction tools, such as VEGA QSARs, within the OECD QSAR toolbox based on chemical smiles and user-guided input.

The Endocrine Disruptome is an open source prediction tool that uses molecular docking to assess the binding of chemicals to 14 different human nuclear receptors, including TRα1 and TRβ1. The tool uses AutoDock Vina for the molecular docking runs through an open source platform called Docking interface for Target Systems.

The Danish (Q)SAR database is a collection of structural information and QSAR predictions developed by the DTU National Food Institute. The database contains multiple QSAR prediction tools for TH-mediated developmental neurotoxicity MIEs, such as TRα1, TRβ1, DIO2 and DIO3.

The MCT8-docking app is a phyton-based notebook that performs a docking simulation of a user-provided ligand against a predicted structure of MCT8. It predicts the likelihood of a ligand binding to MCT8 with an affinity threshold of -9.0 kcal/mol for strong inhibitors.

content.section: Results from the in silico prediction
content.description:
An overview of the in silico predictions is shown in table 1. Most models predicted no effect of silychristin on TR alpha (TRα1) or TR beta (TRβ1): both VEGA QSARs, retrieved from the O-QT agent, and the Endocrine Disruptome indicated no effect of silycrhistin on TRs. Silychristin fell outside the applicability domain for both QSPRpred and the Danish (Q)SAR database TR models, as well as for D2 in the latter model. However, the Danish (Q)SAR database did predict a positive interaction with D3. Additionally, the MCT8-docking APP indicated that silychristin can bind strongly to MCT8, with a binding affinity below the -9.0 kcal/mol threshold for strong inhibitors. Together, these results indicate that the major predicted biological targets of silychristin is on MCT8 and D3.

Table 1. Summary of all in silico predictions for thyroid hormone MIE.

MIE activation in in vitro models.navTitle: MIE activation in in vitro models
MIE activation in in vitro models.navDescription: MIE activation in in vitro models

steps.label: Deiodinase activity
steps.value: Effect of silychristin on deiodinase activity in neural cell lines

steps.label: TH transport and TR activation
steps.value: Effect of silychristin on TH transport and TR activation in neural cell lines

content.section: Using in vitro assays to assess MIE activation by silychristin
content.description: The effect of silychristin on MIE has been tested in three cell lines representing astrocytes (H4), neurons (SK-N-AS) and oligodendrocytes (MO3.13). A TH uptake assay is used to evaluate the effect of silychristin exposure on TH transport in these cell lines. In this assay, the uptake of radioactively labeled T3 and T4 is measured with and without the presence of different concentrations of silychristin. Deiodinase activity was assessed by measuring the transformation of radioactively labeled T3 into TH metabolites in both lysed cells and intact whole cells. TH receptor activity was determined by monitoring changes in the gene expression of KLF9 in SK-N-AS cells.

content.section: Conclusions from the in vitro MIE activation assays
content.description:
An overview of the effects of 10 µM silychristin on TH-related MIEs in physiological relevant neuronal cells is shown in table 1. There was a significant inhibition of T3 transport in all cell models after exposure to silychristin, although the SK-N-AS cell line was less sensitive to silychristin compared to H4 and MO3.13 cells. No direct effect of silychristin was observed on D3 activity, but D3 activity was significant decrease in MO3.13 and SK-N-AS cells whole cell metabolism likely propagated through the decrease uptake of TH. Similarly, a significant decrease of KLF9 expression was observed after exposure to 10 µM silychristin, while no direct effect on TR activity was observed. These results indicate that the major MIE that is affected by silychristin is TH transport.

Table 1. Summary of the effects of 10 µM silychristin on various TH related MIEs in neuronal cell models. Data taken from Dierichs et al. 2026a.

Establishing in vitro point of departure.navTitle: Establishing in vitro point of departure
Establishing in vitro point of departure.navDescription: Establishing in vitro point of departure

steps.label: MCT8 PoD Monte-Carlo simulation
steps.description: not yet on the platform, but refers to 10.5281/zenodo.19665244

steps.label: OATP1C1 PoD Monte-Carlo simulation
steps.description: Simulated distrubtions of PoD for OATP1C1 inhibition

content.section: Generating concentration-response curves for TH uptake in overexpressing cell lines
content.description: To derive relevant point of departure for silchristin specific effects on TH transport, concentration-dependent effects were measured in cell lines overexpressing specific TH transporters. An MCT8 and OATP1C1-overexpressing cell lines were generated and the TH transport in these assays was measured after exposure to multiple concentrations of silychristin. A benchmark concentration (BMC) was derived from these assays based on a benchmark response of 20%, i.e. the lowest observed effect concentration in the in vitro assays. These BMC20 values were then used together with a Monte-Carlo simulatin approach to generate a distribution of possible in vitro point of departures (PoDs).

content.section: Silychristin effects on TH transport mediated by MCT8
content.description:
In this assay, the inhibition of triiodothyronine (T3) uptake via the MCT8 transporter by potential endocrine disrupting chemicals is measured. The assay uses MDCK1 cells stably transfected with human MCT8 combined with a non-radioactive TH uptake assay that utilizes the Sandell-Kolthoff reaction for indirect quantification of TH. Key findings from the assay indicate a dose-dependent inhibitory effect of silychristin on T3 uptake in MCT8-MDCK1 cells, see figure 1. This demonstrates that silychristin effectively inhibits MCT8-mediated T3 transport with a BMC20 (comparable to a LOAEL) of 0.14 µM. The results from this assay provide a possible point of departure for the MCT8-inhibiting capacity of silychristin.

Figure 1. Concentration-response curves for silychristin inhibition on MCT8-mediated T3 uptake. The fraction of T3 uptake is normalized to the solvent control (upper limit = 1.0) and to the positive control (lower limit = 0.0). Blue dots indicate the plate medians from three independent experiments tested in triplicates per plate. The black line indicates the best-fit concentrations response curve, the dotted line the corresponding 95% confidence interval, and the BMC20, the concentration that inhibits the T3 uptake by 20%.

content.section: Silychristin effect on TH transport mediated by OATP1C1
content.description:
In this assay, the inhibition of thyroxine uptake via the OATP1C1 transporter by potential endocrine disrupting chemicals is measured. The uptake of radioactive labelled T4 isotopes is measured in CHOK1 cells stably transfected with human OATP1C1. Key findings from the assay indicate a dose-dependent inhibitory effect of silychristin on T4 uptake in OATP1C1-CHOK1 cells, see figure 2. This demonstrates that silychristin inhibits OATP1C1-mediated T4 transport in a dose-dependent manner with a BMC20 (comparable to a NOAEL) of 3.78 µM. Inhibition of OATP1C1-mediated T4 transport only occurred at high concentrations of silychristin, which indicates that silychristin is a weak inhibitor of OATP1C1-mediated T4 transport.

Figure 2. Concentration-response curves for silychristin inhibition on OATP1C1-mediated T4 uptake. The fraction of T4 uptake is normalized to the solvent control (upper limit = 1.0) and to the positive control (lower limit = 0.0). Blue dots indicate the plate medians from three independent experiments tested in triplicates per plate. The black line indicates the best-fit concentrations response curve, the dotted line the corresponding 95% confidence interval, and the BMC20, the concentration that inhibits the T4 uptake by 20%.

content.section: Monte-carlo simulation of in vitro PoD distributions
content.description:
To propagate the uncertainty that exists within the in vitro assays, we performed a Monte-Carlo simulation of the BMC20 values to produce a distribution of possible in vitro PoDs. In this simulation 10.000 possible in vitro PoDs were sampled using a logistic distribution with the center and spread determined by the mean and 95% confidence intervals of the BMC20 values for each in vitro assays. The resulting distribution of in vitro Pods can be found in Figure 3. These distributions can be used to sample a range of possible in vitro PoDs, instead of a single value, preserving the uncertainty that is present in the vitro assays.

Figure 3. Distribution of in vitro PoDs for MCT8 and OATP1C1, derived from a Monte Carlo simulation. Each bar represents the frequency of sampled in vitro PoD values, with the x-axis displaying the concentration (µM) on a logarithmic scale. The median PoD is indicated by a dashed blue line, while the 5th and 95th percentiles are marked by red and purple dashed lines, respectively.

Effect on KLF9 and BDNF gene expression.navTitle: Effect on KLF9 and BDNF gene expression
Effect on KLF9 and BDNF gene expression.navDescription: Effect on KLF9 and BDNF gene expression

steps.label: KLF9 and BDNF gene expression
steps.description: Dataset for qPCR in neuronal stem cells

content.description:
To determine the direct effect of varying T3 concentrations on KLF9 and BDNF gene expression, KLF9 and BDNF expression in the NPT was measured using qPCR after exposure to 0-100 nM T3 for 10 days during differentiation. The changes in gene expression for KLF9 and BDNF can be found in figure 1. A concentration-response effect was observed for KLF9 with increasing expression of KLF9 at high concentrations of T3. No significant changes were observed for BDNF expression at any concentrations. The concentration-dependent effects of KLF9 gene expression were suitable to be used in the qAOP.

Figure 1. Gene expression of KLF9 and BDNF in hNPT incubated with 0-100 nM T3 for 10 days. Data are normalized to the geometric mean of GUSB and HPRT1 and represented as fold change vs. solvent control as mean ± SD of one biological experiment with six technical replicates. Statistical analysis was performed using an one-way ANOVA with Dunnett’s multiple comparison test. ***p<0.001, ****p<0.0001.

Since BDNF did not show any concentration-dependent effects in gene expression after T3 exposure, the BDNF protein levels in the hNPT were measured using a human BDNF enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit. Protein levels of BDNF were significantly elevated after exposure to 0.3 and 3 nM T3 conditions in the hNPT, but not after exposure to the highest concentration 30 nM T3, see figure 2. Because there were no obvious concentration-dependent effects for BDNF expression, we decided not to include this KE in the eventual modelling of the qAOP.

Figure 2. Protein levels of BDNF in hNPT protein extract after 10 days of differentiation corrected for the amount of DNA. Data is represented as the distribution of replicates from one independent experiment. Statistical significance was tested using an one-way ANOVA with Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test (**p<0.01).

steps.label: Neuronal network activity
steps.descriptions: Generated MEA data of silychristin in neuronal stem cells

content.description:
Neuronal network activity is used as a proxy to derive the effects of varying T3 concentrations on synaptogenesis as a downstream key event. To measure neuronal network activity human neuronal progenitor cells (NPCs) were cultured on a microelectrode array (MEA) plate, which contain small electrodes that can measure the spontaneous activity of neuronal cells. These NPCs were exposed to a concentration-range of 0-100 nM T3 during a differentiation period of 43 days. The neuronal network activity is measured as the mean spike ratio, which describes the activation of individual electrodes, and the mean burst ratio, which describes the activation of multiple electrodes at the same time. The area under the curve values of the mean spike rate showed an initial decrease in spontaneous activity after exposure to 0.3 nM T3. The spontaneous activity increased continuously from 0.3 nM T3 at higher concentration and was significantly higher than the control after exposure to 10, 30 and 100 nM T3. No significant differences were observed between concentrations for mean burst rate, but the mean burst rate did show the same trend as the MSR. The MEA data of the mean spike rate can therefore be used to model the effects of varying T3 concentrations in the qAOP.

Figure 3. MEA measurements of the MSR (A) and the MBR (B) over time after exposure to 0-100 nM T3 in hNPT. Measurements were performed from day 3 until day 43. Results are from one independent experiment with 5-6 replicates per condition. Data is shown as the mean ± SD.
